title: Catapodium coromandelianum
intro: Catapodium coromandelianum, also known as the Coromandel panic grass or Indian panic grass, is a species of perennial grass native to tropical Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with traditional uses including the treatment of fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, as well as its potential to act as an anti-inflammatory and antioxidant. The bioactive constituents of Catapodium coromandelianum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are believed to be responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often made into teas, infusions, or decoctions, and is also used to make powders and capsules for the treatment of various health conditions.
taxonomy: Catapodium coromandelianum is classified as a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It is a part of the subfamily Pooideae and is native to tropical regions. Within the genus Catapodium, it is one of several species found primarily in Asia. The specific classification is further detailed in the binomial nomenclature as Catapodium coromandelianum, with the genus and species names being used to uniquely identify the plant. Further classification may also include its order, class, and kingdom, but the above classification provides a general understanding of its taxonomic position.

The plant is also believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its medicinal applications.
